title: Approach to the Patient with Shock
authors: Rebecca M. Baron, Anthony F. Massaro

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Shock | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF SHOCK
items.title: Understanding Cellular Oxygen Imbalance in Shock
items.content: Shock is characterized by a critical imbalance between cellular oxygen supply and demand, leading to tissue hypoxia. This condition can result from impaired oxygen delivery due to circulatory failure or increased oxygen consumption/utilization issues.
keyPoints: Organ dysfunction in shock results from an imbalance between cellular oxygen supply and demand.
Shock is a common reason for ICU admission and requires prompt identification and treatment.
Inadequate oxygen delivery forces cells into anaerobic metabolism, producing less ATP (per mole of glucose) compared to aerobic metabolism.
ATP-dependent processes consume 20–80% of the cell's energy, crucial for maintaining cellular homeostasis.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · Approach to the Patient with Shock | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF SHOCK

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Shock | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF SHOCK
items.title: Consequences of Inadequate Oxygen Delivery in Shock
items.content: In shock, insufficient oxygen supply disrupts ATP production, impairing cellular functions and leading to organ damage. This can result from impaired oxygen delivery or utilization.
keyPoints: Insufficient oxygen forces cells into anaerobic metabolism, reducing ATP generation significantly.
Decreased ATP affects ion pumping systems like Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase, disrupting osmotic, ionic, and pH homeostasis.
Calcium influx during hypoxia activates enzymes causing cellular swelling and death.
Cellular damage leads to organ system-level effects through inflammatory cascades and microvascular changes.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · Approach to the Patient with Shock | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF SHOCK

items.subtopic: DETERMINANTS OF OXYGEN DELIVERY | SV $ \alpha $ (Preload $ \times $ Contractility)/SVR
items.title: Understanding Oxygen Delivery in Shock: The Role of Stroke Volume
items.content: Oxygen delivery is crucial for preventing shock, and stroke volume (SV) plays a key role. It's determined by preload, contractility, and systemic vascular resistance (SVR). Preload refers to the ventricular end-diastolic volume, while contractility is the heart's ability to contract independently of these factors. SVR represents the afterload or force against which the heart contracts.
keyPoints: Oxygen delivery ($ DO_{2} $) = CO x CaO_{2}
CO = HR x SV
SV $ \alpha $ (Preload $ \times $ Contractility)/SVR
CaO_{2} (in mL/dL) = (Hb x 1.34 x SaO_{2}) + (PaO_{2} x 0.003)
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · DETERMINANTS OF OXYGEN DELIVERY | SV  $ \alpha $ (Preload  $ \times $ Contractility)/SVR

items.subtopic: DETERMINANTS OF OXYGEN DELIVERY | SV $ \alpha $ (Preload $ \times $ Contractility)/SVR
items.title: Components of Arterial Oxygen Content in Shock Management
items.content: Arterial oxygen content (CaO2) is a critical component of oxygen delivery, composed of hemoglobin-bound and dissolved oxygen. It's calculated using the formula: CaO_{2} = (Hb x 1.34 x SaO_{2}) + (PaO_{2} x 0.003). Understanding these components helps in assessing how disease processes might impair oxygen delivery.
keyPoints: CaO_{2} is crucial for $ DO_{2} $
Alterations in Hb, SaO_{2}, or PaO_{2} can reduce oxygen delivery
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · DETERMINANTS OF OXYGEN DELIVERY | SV  $ \alpha $ (Preload  $ \times $ Contractility)/SVR

items.subtopic: CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 1)
items.title: Distributive Shock: Key Features and Management
items.content: Distributive shock is characterized by a primary reduction in systemic vascular resistance (SVR) leading to reduced oxygen delivery. Unlike other types of shock, it features a compensatory increase in cardiac output (CO). Common causes include sepsis, anaphylaxis, severe burns, and adrenal insufficiency.
keyPoints: Primary disturbance: reduction in SVR
Compensatory increase in CO
Common causes: sepsis, anaphylaxis, pancreatitis, severe burns, liver failure, adrenal insufficiency
Sepsis redefined as dysregulated host response to infection with life-threatening organ dysfunction
Septic shock criteria: persistent hypotension requiring vasopressor support despite adequate volume resuscitation and elevated lactate level
Anaphylaxis involves venous and arterial vasodilation, potentially causing up to 35% blood extravasation within 10 minutes
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 1)

items.subtopic: CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 1)
items.title: Cardiogenic Shock: Hemodynamic Profile
items.content: Cardiogenic shock results from a primary cardiac problem leading to reduced cardiac output. It is marked by an increase in systemic vascular resistance as compensation. The hemodynamic profile varies depending on whether the left or right ventricle is affected, influencing pulmonary capillary wedge pressure (PCWP) and central venous pressure (CVP).
keyPoints: Primary problem: reduction in CO due to cardiac issues
Compensatory increase in SVR
Left ventricular involvement: elevated PCWP
Right ventricular involvement: elevated CVP
Causes include myocardial infarction, ischemic cardiomyopathies, primary myocarditis, and mechanical valvular disease
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 1)

items.subtopic: CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 1)
items.title: Hypovolemic Shock: Causes and Characteristics
items.content: Hypovolemic shock is caused by a reduction in intravascular volume, leading to decreased preload and cardiac output. It features elevated systemic vascular resistance and low central venous pressure (CVP) and pulmonary capillary wedge pressure (PCWP). Common causes include hemorrhage, severe vomiting or diarrhea, renal losses, and skin loss.
keyPoints: Primary issue: reduction in intravascular volume
Characterized by elevated SVR and low CVP/PCWP
Common causes: hemorrhage (external/internal), GI illnesses, renal losses, severe burns
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 1)

items.subtopic: CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 2)
items.title: Understanding Obstructive Shock: Causes and Implications
items.content: Obstructive shock results from extracardiac processes that impede blood flow, reducing cardiac output. Key causes include tension pneumothorax, cardiac tamponade, restrictive pericarditis, pulmonary embolism, venous air embolism, fat embolism (right heart), and aortic dissection (left heart). Recognizing these conditions is crucial for timely intervention.
keyPoints: Obstructive shock involves reduced CO due to extracardiac processes.
Causes include tension pneumothorax, cardiac tamponade, restrictive pericarditis, pulmonary embolism, venous air embolism, fat embolism (right heart), aortic dissection (left heart).
Epidemiology: 0.9% of ED shock cases are obstructive.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 2)

items.subtopic: CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 2)
items.title: Mixed Shock and Mortality Rates in Different Settings
items.content: Shock types can overlap, leading to mixed shock presentations. For instance, sepsis may initially cause distributive shock but evolve into mixed shock due to sepsis-induced cardiomyopathy. Mortality rates vary by shock type; septic and cardiogenic shocks have the highest mortality in both ED and ICU settings.
keyPoints: Mixed shock can occur when more than one shock type is present, such as distributive shock evolving into mixed shock due to sepsis-induced cardiomyopathy.
Epidemiology: In the ED, 30.8% hypovolemic, 27.2% septic, 23.4% distributive nonseptic, 14% cardiogenic, 0.9% obstructive.
In ICU settings, 62% septic shock, 16% hypovolemic, 15% cardiogenic, 2% obstructive.
Mortality: ED study shows 56.2% for septic and 52.3% for cardiogenic shock at 90 days.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 2)

items.subtopic: ■ STAGES OF SHOCK | TABLE 314-3 Key Principles in the Treatment of Shock
items.title: Recognizing and Treating Compensated Shock to Prevent Progression
items.content: During compensated shock, the body employs physiological responses to maintain perfusion and oxygen delivery. There are no overt signs of significant organ dysfunction at this stage, but laboratory evaluations may show mild elevations in markers like creatinine or troponin, and lactate levels. Timely intervention can reverse potential organ dysfunction before progression to irreversible shock.
keyPoints: Compensated shock involves physiological responses to maintain perfusion without overt signs of significant organ dysfunction.
Laboratory evaluation may show mild elevations in markers such as creatinine or troponin, and lactate.
Early intervention can reverse potential organ dysfunction.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · ■ STAGES OF SHOCK | TABLE 314-3 Key Principles in the Treatment of Shock

items.subtopic: ■ STAGES OF SHOCK | TABLE 314-3 Key Principles in the Treatment of Shock
items.title: Key Principles for Effective Management of Shock
items.content: The management of shock involves early recognition, assessment of the type of shock, and simultaneous initiation of therapy while evaluating its etiology. Involving a multidisciplinary team is crucial to restore oxygen delivery effectively. Identifying specific etiologies that require additional interventions is essential for patient survival.
keyPoints: Recognize shock early to initiate timely intervention.
Assess the type of shock and begin therapy concurrently with evaluation.
Involve a multidisciplinary team in management.
Aim to restore oxygen delivery as the primary therapeutic goal.
Identify specific etiologies that necessitate additional lifesaving interventions.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · ■ STAGES OF SHOCK | TABLE 314-3 Key Principles in the Treatment of Shock

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 1)
items.title: Early Recognition and High Suspicion for Shock
items.content: The clinician must maintain a high clinical suspicion for shock in patients presenting with new organ dysfunction or hypotension. Early recognition is crucial due to the reversible nature of early organ dysfunction.
keyPoints: Shock should be considered in all patients with new organ dysfunction or hypotension.
Early identification of shock is essential for effective management.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 1)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 1)
items.title: Concurrent Evaluation and Therapy Initiation
items.content: While determining the etiology of shock, therapy should not be delayed. The evaluation of shock etiology and initiation of treatment must occur simultaneously to ensure timely care.
keyPoints: Initiate therapy without delay while evaluating shock etiology.
Evaluation and treatment should proceed concurrently.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 1)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 1)
items.title: History Taking in Shock Evaluation
items.content: A concise, focused history is critical. If the patient cannot provide a history, gather information from family, EMS, or chart reviews. Look for nonspecific symptoms like weakness and focal symptoms that may indicate inadequate oxygen delivery.
keyPoints: Obtain ancillary information if the patient cannot provide a history.
Identify new organ dysfunction through altered mental status or oliguria.
Focal symptoms may relate to primary shock process or tissue hypoxia.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 1)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 2)
items.title: Understanding Vital Signs in Shock
items.content: Vital signs such as hypotension, tachycardia, and tachypnea are common indicators of shock but must be interpreted in context. Hypotension is defined by SBP <90 mmHg or MAP <65 mmHg, yet these values alone do not confirm shock without considering the patient's baseline blood pressure and hemodynamic trends.
keyPoints: Hypotension: SBP <90 mmHg or MAP <65 mmHg
Tachycardia is common but absent in patients with cardiac conduction issues or on nodal blocking medications
Tachypnea indicates early compensatory response to metabolic acidosis
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 2)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 2)
items.title: Assessing Organ Dysfunction in Shock
items.content: Physical examination can reveal organ dysfunction through CNS, renal, and skin assessments. Confusion indicates decreased cerebral perfusion; oliguria (<0.5 mL/kg per h) suggests renal impairment; cold, clammy skin and delayed CRT indicate hypoperfusion.
keyPoints: CNS: Confusion signals decompensated shock
Renal: Oliguria <0.5 mL/kg per h indicates possible shock
Skin: Cold, clammy skin and delayed CRT suggest hypoperfusion
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 2)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 2)
items.title: Differentiating Types of Shock by Physical Examination
items.content: Physical examination helps distinguish between high-output (distributive) and low-output (cardiogenic, hypovolemic, obstructive) shock. Warm extremities and large pulse pressure suggest distributive shock; cool extremities and narrow pulse pressure indicate low CO shock. Elevated IVP (>8 cm) suggests cardiogenic or obstructive shock, while reduced IVP (<8 cm) indicates hypovolemic shock.
keyPoints: High-output (distributive): Warm extremities, large pulse pressure
Low-output: Cool extremities, narrow pulse pressure
Elevated IVP (>8 cm): Cardiogenic or obstructive shock
Reduced IVP (<8 cm): Hypovolemic shock
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 2)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 3)
items.title: Shock Index as a Predictor for Critical Bleeding
items.content: The Shock Index (SI), defined as heart rate divided by systolic blood pressure, is used to identify patients at risk of critical bleeding. An elevated SI (>0.9) indicates a higher likelihood of needing transfusion and identifies those with significant hemorrhagic shock more effectively than heart rate or blood pressure alone.
keyPoints: Shock Index (SI) = HR/SBP
Normal SI: 0.5–0.7
Elevated SI (>0.9) indicates critical bleeding risk and transfusion requirement
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 3)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 3)
items.title: qSOFA Score for Identifying Severe Sepsis
items.content: The quick Sequential Organ Failure Assessment (qSOFA) score is a rapid assessment tool used to identify patients with sepsis at high risk of mortality or prolonged ICU stay. It assigns points for systolic blood pressure <100, respiratory rate >22, or altered mental status (Glasgow Coma Scale <15). A qSOFA ≥2 suggests significant risk.
keyPoints: qSOFA score components: SBP <100, RR >22, GCS <15
qSOFA ≥2 indicates higher risk of death or prolonged ICU stay
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 3)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 3)
items.title: Role of Lactate in Shock Management
items.content: Lactate measurement is crucial for diagnosing, risk stratifying, and treating shock. Elevated lactate levels indicate anaerobic metabolism due to hypoxia, correlating with worse outcomes in shock patients. Serial lactate measurements help evaluate critically ill patients' response to therapy.
keyPoints: Increased lactate indicates anaerobic metabolism
Lactate correlates with worse outcomes in shock
Serial lactate measurements are valuable for monitoring treatment response
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 3)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 4)
items.title: Role of Electrocardiogram in Shock Evaluation
items.content: The electrocardiogram (ECG) is crucial for identifying arrhythmias and myocardial infarction, which can contribute to shock. Specific patterns like S1 Q3 T3 suggest pulmonary embolism, while reduced voltage with electrical alternans indicates pericardial tamponade.
keyPoints: Bradycardic or tachycardic arrhythmias reduce cardiac output (CO).
ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction can be identified on ECG.
S1 Q3 T3 pattern raises concerns for pulmonary embolism.
Reduced voltage with electrical alternans suggests pericardial tamponade.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 4)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 4)
items.title: Chest X-Ray in Shock Diagnosis
items.content: The chest x-ray (CXR) helps identify infectious processes, cardiogenic causes, and pneumothorax. However, it is not sensitive or specific for pulmonary embolism, which may require a CT angiogram.
keyPoints: New focal alveolar or interstitial infiltrate suggests infectious process/distributive septic shock.
Bilateral cephalization of pulmonary vasculature indicates cardiogenic pulmonary edema.
CXR is not sensitive/specific for pulmonary embolism; CT angiogram may be needed.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 4)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 4)
items.title: Point-of-Care Ultrasound in Shock Evaluation
items.content: POCUS is valuable for assessing cardiac function, intravascular volume status, and identifying fluid collections. It aids in differentiating shock types but does not improve survival outcomes.
keyPoints: POCUS protocols like RUSH, ACES, SESAME assess cardiac function and volume status.
TTE evaluates LV and RV function; identifies pericardial tamponade.
IVC evaluation helps assess intravascular filling; collapsible IVC suggests reduced volume.
2018 study showed no survival benefit at 30 days with POCUS in undifferentiated shock.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 4)

items.subtopic: INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 1)
items.title: Importance of Early Intravenous Access and Monitoring
items.content: Initiating treatment for shock requires simultaneous efforts to identify its etiology. Ensuring adequate intravenous access is crucial, with two peripheral venous catheters recommended for initial volume resuscitation in distributive or hypovolemic shock. For ongoing hypotension despite resuscitation, a central venous catheter (CVC) facilitates vasopressor therapy and hemodynamic monitoring.
keyPoints: Two peripheral venous catheters (16 or 18 gauge) for initial access
Central venous catheter (CVC) indicated for ongoing hypotension despite resuscitation
CVC provides therapy with vasopressors, inotropes, and hemodynamic monitoring
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 1)

items.subtopic: INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 1)
items.title: Volume Resuscitation Goals and Strategies
items.content: The aim of initial volume resuscitation is to restore tissue perfusion by moving the patient to the non-preload-dependent portion of the Starling curve. A minimum of 30 mL/kg fluid resuscitation is recommended for suspected septic shock, as per the Surviving Sepsis Campaign guidelines.
keyPoints: Goal: Move patient to non-preload-dependent portion of Starling curve
Minimum initial volume resuscitation for septic shock: 30 mL/kg
Crystalloid commonly used; packed red blood cells for hemorrhagic shock
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 1)

items.subtopic: INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 1)
items.title: Considerations for Red Blood Cell Transfusion in Shock Management
items.content: In cases of hypovolemic shock due to ongoing hemorrhage, packed red blood cells are warranted. For massive transfusion scenarios, platelets and fresh frozen plasma should be administered to counteract component dilution. Red cell administration may also optimize oxygen delivery if hemoglobin is <7 g/dL.
keyPoints: Packed red blood cells for hypovolemic shock due to hemorrhage
Platelets and fresh frozen plasma in massive transfusion scenarios
Red cell administration considered if hemoglobin <7 g/dL
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 1)

items.subtopic: INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 2)
items.title: Predicting Volume Responsiveness with the Passive Leg Raise Test
items.content: The passive leg raise (PLR) test is used to predict responsiveness to additional IV fluid by providing an endogenous volume bolus. The patient is positioned at a 45-degree angle, and changes in cardiac output or pulse pressure variation are assessed within one minute.
keyPoints: A >12% SV variation suggests a volume-responsive state.
Measurement requires the patient to be in a volume-controlled mode of ventilation without arrhythmias.
PLR test is useful for assessing volume responsiveness in mechanically ventilated patients.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 2)

items.subtopic: INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 2)
items.title: Utilizing Echocardiography and PAC in Volume Status Assessment
items.content: Echocardiography, including parameters like IVC diameter and LV function, helps assess intravascular fluid status. Pulmonary artery catheterization provides direct measurements of CVP and PCWP, useful in mixed shock or unclear etiology cases.
keyPoints: IVC diameter and collapse are commonly used echocardiographic parameters for volume assessment.
PAC measures include CVP, PA pressure, and PCWP as a surrogate for LA pressure.
Routine PAC use does not show mortality benefit but is useful in mixed or unclear shock etiology.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 2)

items.subtopic: INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 2)
items.title: Vasopressor and Inotropic Support in Shock Management
items.content: After optimizing intravascular volume, vasopressors or inotropes are used for persistent hypotension. Norepinephrine is preferred in septic shock; dobutamine is first-line for cardiogenic shock.
keyPoints: Norepinephrine is the first-choice vasopressor in septic shock due to its $\alpha_{1}$ and $\beta_{1}$ effects.
Vasopressin can be used as a second agent in septic shock, though it did not show mortality reduction in trials.
Dobutamine is preferred for cardiogenic shock due to its $\beta$-mediated effects.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 2)

items.subtopic: OXYGENATION AND VENTILATION SUPPORT
items.title: Initiate and Titrate Oxygen Therapy in Shock to Maintain Target SpO2 Levels
items.content: In shock, hypoxemia can arise from various causes including pulmonary processes or pulmonary edema. Supplemental oxygen should be initiated promptly and titrated to maintain an $SpO_{2}$ of 92–95%. This may necessitate intubation and mechanical ventilation if hypoxemia persists.
keyPoints: Supplemental oxygen should be used to maintain $SpO_{2}$ levels between 92–95%.
Intubation and mechanical ventilation might be required to achieve target oxygen saturation.
Prompt initiation of these measures is crucial to minimize tissue hypoxia duration.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · OXYGENATION AND VENTILATION SUPPORT

items.subtopic: OXYGENATION AND VENTILATION SUPPORT
items.title: Use Lung-Protective Ventilation Strategies in Shock Patients Requiring Mechanical Support
items.content: When mechanical ventilation is initiated for shock patients, it should be done using lung-protective strategies. These include low tidal volume ventilation and optimizing positive end-expiratory pressure to minimize ventilator-induced lung injury.
keyPoints: Mechanical ventilation should employ lung-protective strategies.
Low tidal volume ventilation helps reduce the risk of ventilator-induced lung injury.
Optimizing positive end-expiratory pressure is crucial in minimizing further lung damage.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · OXYGENATION AND VENTILATION SUPPORT

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Shock | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF SHOCK
items.question: Which of the following is a common cause of impaired oxygen utilization leading to shock?
items.explanation: Cyanide poisoning causes uncoupling of oxidative phosphorylation, impairing oxygen utilization.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · Approach to the Patient with Shock | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF SHOCK

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Shock | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF SHOCK
items.question: What metabolic pathway is primarily affected by insufficient oxygen supply in shock?
items.explanation: Insufficient oxygen forces cells into anaerobic metabolism, affecting oxidative phosphorylation.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · Approach to the Patient with Shock | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF SHOCK

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Shock | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF SHOCK
items.question: What percentage of a cell's energy is typically consumed by ATP-dependent ion pumping systems?
items.explanation: ATP-dependent ion pumping systems, such as the Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase, consume 20–80% of a cell's energy.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · Approach to the Patient with Shock | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF SHOCK

items.subtopic: DETERMINANTS OF OXYGEN DELIVERY | SV $ \alpha $ (Preload $ \times $ Contractility)/SVR
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a determinant of stroke volume (SV) according to the passage?
items.explanation: Heart rate is not a determinant of stroke volume; it is a component of cardiac output.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · DETERMINANTS OF OXYGEN DELIVERY | SV  $ \alpha $ (Preload  $ \times $ Contractility)/SVR

items.subtopic: DETERMINANTS OF OXYGEN DELIVERY | SV $ \alpha $ (Preload $ \times $ Contractility)/SVR
items.question: In the context of oxygen delivery, what does preload refer to?
options: The force against which the ventricle must contract
The myocardial fiber length before contraction
The ability of the ventricle to contract independently
The partial pressure of oxygen in arterial blood
items.explanation: Preload refers to the myocardial fiber length before contraction, or ventricular end-diastolic volume.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · DETERMINANTS OF OXYGEN DELIVERY | SV  $ \alpha $ (Preload  $ \times $ Contractility)/SVR

items.subtopic: DETERMINANTS OF OXYGEN DELIVERY | SV $ \alpha $ (Preload $ \times $ Contractility)/SVR
items.question: Which component of arterial oxygen content is directly affected by hemoglobin concentration?
items.explanation: Hemoglobin concentration affects the oxygen carried with convection, which is related to arterial oxygen saturation (SaO2).
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · DETERMINANTS OF OXYGEN DELIVERY | SV  $ \alpha $ (Preload  $ \times $ Contractility)/SVR

items.subtopic: CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 1)
items.question: Which type of shock is characterized by a primary physiologic disturbance that results in reduced systemic vascular resistance (SVR) and an increase in cardiac output (CO)?
items.explanation: Distributive shock is characterized by reduced SVR and a compensatory increase in CO.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 1)

items.subtopic: CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 1)
items.question: Which condition is most commonly associated with distributive shock?
items.explanation: The most common cause of distributive shock is sepsis.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 1)

items.subtopic: CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 1)
items.question: In cardiogenic shock, what hemodynamic change typically occurs when the left ventricle is affected?
options: Elevation of pulmonary capillary wedge pressure
items.explanation: When the left ventricle is affected, there will be an elevation of the pulmonary capillary wedge pressure.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 1)

items.subtopic: CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 1)
items.question: Which type of shock is characterized by reduced cardiac output due to a reduction in preload?
items.explanation: Hypovolemic shock is characterized by reduced cardiac output due to a reduction in preload.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 1)

items.subtopic: CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 2)
items.question: Which of the following conditions can cause obstructive shock by impeding venous return to the heart?
items.explanation: Tension pneumothorax can impede venous return to the heart, causing obstructive shock.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 2)

items.subtopic: CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 2)
items.question: In which clinical setting does septic shock most commonly predominate?
items.explanation: Septic shock predominates in the ICU setting.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 2)

items.subtopic: CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 2)
items.question: What type of shock is most prevalent among patients in cardiac ICUs according to a 2019 study?
items.explanation: A 2019 study found that 66% of patients in cardiac ICUs had cardiogenic shock.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 2)

items.subtopic: ■ STAGES OF SHOCK | TABLE 314-3 Key Principles in the Treatment of Shock
items.question: Which stage of shock is characterized by no overt signs of significant organ dysfunction but may show mild laboratory abnormalities?
items.explanation: During compensated shock, the body attempts to maintain adequate perfusion and oxygen delivery with no overt signs of significant organ dysfunction.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · ■ STAGES OF SHOCK | TABLE 314-3 Key Principles in the Treatment of Shock

items.subtopic: ■ STAGES OF SHOCK | TABLE 314-3 Key Principles in the Treatment of Shock
items.question: What is the primary aim of therapy during the initial phases of shock?
items.explanation: The aim of therapy is to restore perfusion and oxygen delivery during the initial phases of shock.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · ■ STAGES OF SHOCK | TABLE 314-3 Key Principles in the Treatment of Shock

items.subtopic: ■ STAGES OF SHOCK | TABLE 314-3 Key Principles in the Treatment of Shock
items.question: Which stage of shock is characterized by permanent organ dysfunction?
items.explanation: In irreversible shock, organ dysfunction becomes permanent and often progresses to multisystem organ dysfunction.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · ■ STAGES OF SHOCK | TABLE 314-3 Key Principles in the Treatment of Shock

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 1)
items.question: What are the two specific aims of the initial evaluation of a patient with shock?
options: Confirmation of the presence of shock and identification of the type or etiology of shock.
Initiation of therapy without further diagnostic testing.
Assessment of long-term prognosis and treatment planning.
items.explanation: The initial evaluation aims to confirm shock presence and identify its type or etiology.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 1)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following is an essential aspect when obtaining a history from a patient with suspected shock?
options: Focusing solely on past medical history.
Ignoring nonspecific symptoms like weakness or malaise.
Determining if focal symptoms are related to primary process causing shock or inadequate oxygen delivery.
Delaying therapy until the final diagnosis is determined.
items.explanation: It's crucial to determine whether focal symptoms relate to the primary process causing shock or inadequate oxygen delivery.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 1)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following is an example of a symptom that may suggest distributive shock from sepsis?
options: Fever and history suggesting a focal site of infection.
items.explanation: Distributive shock from sepsis may present with fever and a history suggesting a focal site of infection.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 1)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 1)
items.question: What should be done if the patient is unable to provide a history?
options: Obtain ancillary information from family, EMS personnel, or chart review.
Only rely on physical examination findings.
Delay evaluation until the patient can communicate.
items.explanation: If a patient cannot provide history, obtain information from family, EMS, or perform a brief chart review.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 1)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 2)
items.question: Which vital sign abnormality is a common early compensatory mechanism in shock?
items.explanation: Tachycardia is a common compensatory mechanism in shock.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 2)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 2)
items.question: What does cold and clammy skin indicate in the context of shock?
items.explanation: Cold and clammy skin is a sign of hypoperfusion with compensatory vasoconstriction.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 2)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 2)
items.question: What does an elevated jugular venous pressure (IVP) suggest in the context of shock?
options: Obstructive shock with high right-sided cardiac pressures
Low CO due to peripheral vasodilation
items.explanation: Elevation of IVP is seen with high right-sided cardiac pressures, as in cardiogenic or obstructive shock.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 2)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 2)
items.question: Which finding would suggest low CO forms of shock on physical examination?
options: Cool extremities with narrow pulse pressure
items.explanation: Cool extremities with narrow pulse pressure indicate low CO forms of shock.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 2)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 3)
items.question: Which clinical score is used to identify high-risk patients with sepsis?
items.explanation: The qSOFA score is a rapid assessment scale for identifying critically ill patients with sepsis.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 3)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 3)
items.question: What does an elevated shock index (>0.9) indicate in hypovolemic shock?
items.explanation: An elevated SI indicates critical bleeding and the need for transfusion in hypovolemic shock.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 3)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 3)
items.question: What does an elevation of lactate levels indicate in a patient with shock?
options: Reduced oxygen delivery and cellular hypoxia
items.explanation: Elevated lactate indicates reduced oxygen delivery and cellular hypoxia.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 3)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 3)
items.question: Which laboratory test can help identify a source of infection in distributive shock?
items.explanation: Elevation of alkaline phosphatase may suggest biliary obstruction and identify a source of infection.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 3)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 4)
items.question: Which ECG finding would raise concerns for pulmonary embolism in a patient with shock?
items.explanation: The S1 Q3 T3 pattern on ECG is associated with pulmonary embolism.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 4)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 4)
items.question: What does a collapsible inferior vena cava (IVC) suggest in spontaneously breathing patients?
items.explanation: A collapsible IVC suggests reduced intravascular volume.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 4)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 4)
items.question: Which imaging modality may be needed to exclude the diagnosis of pulmonary embolism?
items.explanation: A chest CT angiogram is needed to exclude pulmonary embolism.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 4)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 4)
items.question: What does the presence of reduced voltage with electrical alternans on an ECG suggest?
items.explanation: Reduced voltage with electrical alternans suggests pericardial tamponade.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 4)

items.subtopic: INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 1)
items.question: What is the initial step in managing a patient with undifferentiated shock?
options: Initiate treatment for circulatory shock while identifying its etiology.
Wait until the specific type of shock is identified before starting treatment.
Begin antibiotic therapy immediately regardless of shock type.
Start vasopressor therapy as soon as possible.
items.explanation: Treatment for circulatory shock should be initiated simultaneously with efforts to identify its etiology.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 1)

items.subtopic: INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 1)
items.question: What is the recommended initial volume of resuscitation for patients with suspected septic shock?
items.explanation: The Surviving Sepsis Campaign recommends a minimum of 30 mL/kg as an initial volume of resuscitation for suspected septic shock.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 1)

items.subtopic: INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 1)
items.question: Which device is preferred for obtaining central venous access quickly in critically ill patients?
items.explanation: An intraosseous device is the quickest method for obtaining central access in critically ill patients or during cardiopulmonary arrest.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 1)

items.subtopic: INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 1)
items.question: What type of monitoring can a central venous catheter provide?
items.explanation: A central venous catheter provides both hemodynamic monitoring (CVP) and a means to obtain central venous oxygen saturations (ScvO₂).
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 1)

items.subtopic: INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 2)
items.question: Which test is used to predict responsiveness to additional IV fluid by providing an endogenous volume bolus?
items.explanation: The passive leg raise (PLR) test is used to predict responsiveness to additional IV fluid by providing the patient with an endogenous volume bolus.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 2)

items.subtopic: INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 2)
items.question: What percentage of stroke volume variation suggests a volume-responsive state?
items.explanation: A >12% SV variation suggests a volume-responsive state.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 2)

items.subtopic: INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 2)
items.question: Which vasopressor is considered the first-choice agent in septic shock?
items.explanation: Norepinephrine is the first-choice vasopressor in septic shock.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 2)

items.subtopic: INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 2)
items.question: In which type of shock is dopamine not recommended as a first-line agent?
items.explanation: Dopamine does not have a role as a first-line agent in distributive shock.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 2)

items.subtopic: OXYGENATION AND VENTILATION SUPPORT
items.question: In patients with shock, what is the target range for SpO2 when titrating supplemental oxygen?
items.explanation: Supplemental oxygen should be initiated and titrated to maintain an SpO2 of 92–95%.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · OXYGENATION AND VENTILATION SUPPORT

items.subtopic: OXYGENATION AND VENTILATION SUPPORT
items.question: Which strategy is important when initiating mechanical ventilation support in shock patients?
items.explanation: It is important to provide ventilation with lung-protective strategies focused on low tidal volume ventilation.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · OXYGENATION AND VENTILATION SUPPORT

items.subtopic: OXYGENATION AND VENTILATION SUPPORT
items.question: What should be done daily when a patient is on mechanical ventilation?
options: Cessation of sedation to assess neurologic function
items.explanation: There should be daily sedation cessation to assess underlying neurologic function and minimize time on mechanical ventilation.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · OXYGENATION AND VENTILATION SUPPORT

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Shock | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF SHOCK
items.statement: Shock is always caused by impaired oxygen delivery.
items.explanation: Shock can also develop during states of increased oxygen consumption or impaired oxygen utilization.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · Approach to the Patient with Shock | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF SHOCK

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Shock | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF SHOCK
items.statement: Early shock is often reversible with restoration of adequate oxygen supply.
items.explanation: The organ dysfunction seen in early shock can be reversed if adequate oxygen supply is restored.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · Approach to the Patient with Shock | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF SHOCK

items.subtopic: DETERMINANTS OF OXYGEN DELIVERY | SV $ \alpha $ (Preload $ \times $ Contractility)/SVR
items.statement: Systemic vascular resistance (SVR) is a determinant of stroke volume.
items.explanation: SVR represents afterload, which inversely affects stroke volume.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · DETERMINANTS OF OXYGEN DELIVERY | SV  $ \alpha $ (Preload  $ \times $ Contractility)/SVR

items.subtopic: DETERMINANTS OF OXYGEN DELIVERY | SV $ \alpha $ (Preload $ \times $ Contractility)/SVR
items.statement: Cardiac contractility is dependent on preload and afterload.
items.explanation: Contractility refers to the ability of the ventricle to contract independent of preload and afterload.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · DETERMINANTS OF OXYGEN DELIVERY | SV  $ \alpha $ (Preload  $ \times $ Contractility)/SVR

items.subtopic: CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 1)
items.statement: Anaphylaxis can cause distributive shock through histamine release leading to venous and arterial vasodilation.
items.explanation: Anaphylaxis is a form of distributive shock that involves histamine-mediated vasodilation.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 1)

items.subtopic: CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 1)
items.statement: In cardiogenic shock, the systemic vascular resistance typically decreases.
items.explanation: Cardiogenic shock is characterized by a compensatory increase in systemic vascular resistance.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 1)

items.subtopic: CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 2)
items.statement: Obstructive shock is primarily caused by intracardiac processes.
items.explanation: Obstructive shock is caused by extracardiac pulmonary vascular or mechanical processes.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 2)

items.subtopic: CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 2)
items.statement: In the ED study from Denmark, septic shock had a higher 90-day mortality rate than cardiogenic shock.
items.explanation: Septic shock had a 56.2% 90-day mortality rate compared to 52.3% for cardiogenic shock.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 2)

items.subtopic: ■ STAGES OF SHOCK | TABLE 314-3 Key Principles in the Treatment of Shock
items.statement: Appropriate interventions during compensated and decompensated shock can reverse organ dysfunction.
items.explanation: Interventions to restore perfusion and oxygen delivery during these initial two phases can indeed reverse organ dysfunction.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · ■ STAGES OF SHOCK | TABLE 314-3 Key Principles in the Treatment of Shock

items.subtopic: ■ STAGES OF SHOCK | TABLE 314-3 Key Principles in the Treatment of Shock
items.statement: In early sepsis, there is a compensatory rise in systemic vascular resistance.
items.explanation: In early sepsis with reduction in SVR, there is a compensatory rise in heart rate and cardiac output.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · ■ STAGES OF SHOCK | TABLE 314-3 Key Principles in the Treatment of Shock

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 1)
items.statement: The final diagnosis should be determined before initiating therapy in shock patients.
items.explanation: Therapy initiation should not be delayed while determining the final diagnosis.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 1)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 1)
items.statement: A patient with distributive shock from urosepsis may report chest discomfort due to tissue hypoxia.
items.explanation: Chest discomfort in a patient with distributive shock from urosepsis can be related to tissue hypoxia.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 1)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 2)
items.statement: An elevated heart rate without hypotension can reliably exclude the diagnosis of shock.
items.explanation: An elevated heart rate without hypotension does not rule out shock, as younger patients may compensate for an extended period.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 2)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 2)
items.statement: Capillary refill time is typically normal in the setting of shock.
items.explanation: In shock, capillary refill time is delayed due to hypoperfusion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 2)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 3)
items.statement: A qSOFA score ≥2 is associated with a lower risk of death or prolonged ICU stay.
items.explanation: A qSOFA score ≥2 is associated with a significantly greater risk of death or prolonged ICU stay.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 3)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 3)
items.statement: The shock index is defined as the heart rate divided by systolic blood pressure.
items.explanation: SI is indeed defined as HR/SBP.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 3)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 4)
items.statement: CXR findings are sensitive and specific for pulmonary embolism.
items.explanation: CXR findings are neither sensitive nor specific for pulmonary embolism.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 4)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 4)
items.statement: POCUS can definitively determine the etiology of shock and improve survival.
items.explanation: A study showed no benefit in survival at 30 days or hospital discharge with POCUS for undifferentiated shock.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 4)

items.subtopic: INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 1)
items.statement: In patients with distributive shock, early aggressive volume replacement is not necessary.
items.explanation: Early aggressive volume replacement is well established as necessary for patients with distributive shock.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 1)

items.subtopic: INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 1)
items.statement: An arterial line can provide continuous determination of mean arterial pressure (MAP).
items.explanation: Placement of an arterial line allows for intravascular measurement of blood pressure and continuous determination of MAP.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 1)

items.subtopic: INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 2)
items.statement: Pulmonary artery catheterization has been shown to reduce mortality and length-of-stay in all patients with shock.
items.explanation: Studies have not identified a mortality or length-of-stay benefit with routine use of pulmonary artery catheterization.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 2)

items.subtopic: INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 2)
items.statement: Vasopressin can be used as a second agent for hypotension in septic shock.
items.explanation: Vasopressin is safe and has a role as a second agent for hypotension in septic shock.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 2)

items.subtopic: OXYGENATION AND VENTILATION SUPPORT
items.statement: Noninvasive ventilation is widely supported by data for use in the setting of shock.
items.explanation: There are currently few data to support the use of noninvasive ventilation in the setting of shock.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · OXYGENATION AND VENTILATION SUPPORT

items.subtopic: OXYGENATION AND VENTILATION SUPPORT
items.statement: Obtaining cultures should delay antibiotic administration if septic shock is suspected.
items.explanation: The inability to obtain cultures should not delay the start of treatment for septic shock.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · OXYGENATION AND VENTILATION SUPPORT

items.subtopic: DETERMINANTS OF OXYGEN DELIVERY | SV $ \alpha $ (Preload $ \times $ Contractility)/SVR
items.assertion: A disease process affecting systemic vascular resistance can lead to shock.
items.reason: Systemic vascular resistance is a determinant of stroke volume, which affects oxygen delivery.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · DETERMINANTS OF OXYGEN DELIVERY | SV  $ \alpha $ (Preload  $ \times $ Contractility)/SVR

items.subtopic: CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 1)
items.assertion: Adrenal insufficiency can lead to distributive shock.
items.reason: It causes vasodilation and aldosterone deficiency-mediated hypovolemia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why adrenal insufficiency leads to distributive shock.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 1)

items.subtopic: CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 1)
items.assertion: Hypovolemic shock is primarily caused by a reduction in systemic vascular resistance.
items.reason: It results from processes that reduce intravascular volume, leading to decreased preload.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; hypovolemic shock is due to reduced preload, not reduced SVR.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · CLASSIFICATION OF SHOCK (part 1)

items.subtopic: ■ STAGES OF SHOCK | TABLE 314-3 Key Principles in the Treatment of Shock
items.assertion: Shock progresses through three stages: compensated, decompensated, and irreversible.
items.reason: During compensated shock, there are no overt signs of significant organ dysfunction.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason correctly explains that during the first stage (compensated), organ dysfunction is not yet evident.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · ■ STAGES OF SHOCK | TABLE 314-3 Key Principles in the Treatment of Shock

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 1)
items.assertion: In patients with shock, the history often includes nonspecific symptoms like weakness or malaise.
items.reason: These symptoms are indicative of inadequate oxygen delivery for cellular metabolic needs.
items.explanation: Nonspecific symptoms in shock can indicate inadequate oxygen delivery.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 1)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 1)
items.assertion: A patient with a history of trauma may present with hypovolemic shock.
items.reason: Trauma is unrelated to the development of hypovolemic shock.
items.explanation: Trauma can lead to hypovolemic shock due to blood loss or fluid depletion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 1)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 2)
items.assertion: New encephalopathy in a patient with shock represents decompensated shock.
items.reason: The body redirects blood flow to the CNS early in shock, and altered mental status indicates compensatory mechanisms have been outstripped.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 2)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 2)
items.assertion: Peripheral edema is typically present in acute hypovolemic shock.
items.reason: Patients with cardiogenic shock and right-sided cardiac dysfunction may have peripheral edema, but not usually in acute hypovolemic shock.
items.explanation: A is false, but R is true.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 2)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 3)
items.assertion: An elevated lactate level correlates with a worse outcome in septic shock.
items.reason: Lactate is produced under anaerobic conditions, indicating cellular hypoxia and poor tissue perfusion.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 3)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 3)
items.assertion: A normal shock index is greater than 0.9.
items.reason: The normal range for SI is 0.5–0.7.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; a normal SI is 0.5–0.7, not >0.9.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 3)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 4)
items.assertion: POCUS is valuable in evaluating patients with mixed shock.
items.reason: It provides insight into the relative contribution of individual shock types.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 4)

items.subtopic: EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 4)
items.assertion: The performance of bedside echocardiogram by critical care practitioners replaces formal examination.
items.reason: It is expected that emergency medicine and critical care providers are competent in basic ultrasound techniques.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; bedside echocardiograms do not replace formal examinations.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · EVALUATION OF THE PATIENT WITH SHOCK (part 4)

items.subtopic: INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 1)
items.assertion: In patients with hypovolemic shock due to ongoing hemorrhage, volume replacement with packed red blood cells is warranted.
items.reason: Hemoglobin content is a key determinant of oxygen delivery.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why packed red blood cells are used in hypovolemic shock due to hemorrhage.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 1)

items.subtopic: INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 1)
items.assertion: The use of early goal-directed therapy (EGDT) is universally required for all patients with septic shock.
items.reason: Targeted resuscitation using invasive monitoring has been demonstrated to be necessary in recent studies.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; targeted resuscitation using invasive monitoring is not universally required, although it was part of past EGDT protocols.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 1)

items.subtopic: INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 2)
items.assertion: Norepinephrine should be used first in patients with cardiogenic shock.
items.reason: Dobutamine is the first-line agent for patients with cardiogenic shock due to its primarily beta-mediated effects.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because dobutamine, not norepinephrine, is the first-line agent in cardiogenic shock.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 2)

items.subtopic: INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 2)
items.assertion: Vasopressin deficiency may be present in patients with distributive shock.
items.reason: Vasopressin acts on vasopressin receptors to reverse vasodilation and redistribute flow to the splanchnic circulation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is a correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · INITIAL TREATMENT OF SHOCK (part 2)

items.subtopic: OXYGENATION AND VENTILATION SUPPORT
items.assertion: Patients with shock may require mechanical ventilation due to high minute ventilatory needs.
items.reason: As shock progresses, patients may not be able to maintain adequate respiratory compensation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Shock · OXYGENATION AND VENTILATION SUPPORT
